{{nihongo|'''Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko'''|伊甚国造|''ijimi no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''ijimi kokuzō''}} was a [[kuni no miyatsuko]] who ruled the later [[Hanyu District, Chiba|Hanyu District]] and [[Nagara District, Chiba|Nagara District]] of [[Kazusa Province]] (present-day [[Chōsei District, Chiba|Chōsei District and Municipality]] in [[Chiba Prefecture]]) under the [[ritsuryō provinces|ritsuryō system]]. His [[kabane]] was [[Atai (kabane)|Atai]]. Before the establishment of the {{ill|Ijimi Miyake|ja|伊甚屯倉}}, he is thought to have also ruled {{ill|Isumi District|ja|夷隅郡|lt=Ishumi District}}. As for the writing, the "Kokuzō Hongi" of the {{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}} and the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' both give '''Ijimi''' no Kuni no Miyatsuko (伊甚), while the ''[[Kojiki]]'' gives '''Ijimu''' no Kuni no Miyatsuko (伊自牟; ''ijimu-'').

== Explanation ==
According to the "Kokuzō Hongi" of the ''Sendai Kuji Hongi'', during the reign of [[Emperor Seimu]], Ikoroto-no-Atai, a grandson of Ikohoto-no-Mikoto — ancestor of the {{ill|Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko (Bōsō)|ja|阿波国造|lt=Awa no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} — was appointed Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko.<ref>''Sendai Kuji Hongi'', Vol. 10, "Kokuzō Hongi".</ref> Meanwhile, the ''Kojiki'' names [[Take-Hiratori-no-Mikoto|Take-Hiratori-no-Mikoto]], son of [[Amenohohi|Amenohohi-no-Mikoto]], as the ancestor of the Ijimu no Kuni no Miyatsuko.<ref group="note">The ''Kojiki'' states that Take-Hiratori-no-Mikoto "was the ancestor of the [[Izumo no Kuni no Miyatsuko]], the {{ill|Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武蔵国造|lt=Musashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} (无邪志), the {{ill|Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造|lt=Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, the {{ill|Shimo-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造|lt=Shimo-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}, the Ijimu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, the {{ill|Tsushima Agatanushi|ja|津島県直|lt=Tsushima Agatanushi}}, and the {{ill|Tōtōmi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|遠江国造|lt=Tōtōmi no Kuni no Miyatsuko}}."</ref><ref>''Kojiki'', Volume 1, Amaterasu-Ōmikami and Susanoo-no-Mikoto section.</ref>

The Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko is thought to have ruled the basins of the [[Isumi River]] and the [[Ichinomiya River (Chiba)|Ichinomiya River]]. In the [[Chōnan, Chiba|Chōnan]] area, large [[keyhole-shaped kofun]] from the [[4th century]], such as the [[Nōmanji Kofun]] and [[Aburadono Kofun Cluster#Aburadono No. 1 Kofun|Aburadono No. 1 Kofun]], suggest the existence of a chieftain power in the early [[Kofun period]]. However, from the [[5th century]] onward, no large keyhole-shaped kofun were built.<ref name="千">''Place Names of Chiba Prefecture (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 12)'', p. 840.</ref>

The ''Nihon Shoki'' contains an account of the offering of the Ijimi Miyake. According to the entry for [[Emperor Ankan]] 1 ([[534]]), 4th month, 1st day, {{ill|Kashiwade-no-Ōmaro|ja|膳大麻呂|lt=Kashiwade-no-Omi-Ōmaro}}, the ''Naizen-no-kami'' (head chef), ordered the Ijimi no Kuni no Miyatsuko to offer pearls. When the miyatsuko was late with the offering and they tried to arrest him, the miyatsuko [[Ijimi Wakago]] fled and hid in the sleeping quarters of [[Kasuga-no-Yamada-no-Himemiko|Kasuga-no-Yamada]] Empress. The empress was so startled that she fainted, aggravating his offense; to atone, Wakago offered the Ijimi Miyake to Kasuga-no-Yamada Empress, and this is said to have become the later Ishumi District.<ref>''Nihon Shoki'', Vol. 18, Emperor Ankan 1, 4th month, 1st day entry.</ref> Since the text says "now it has been divided into a district", it must have been a vast miyake whose territory extended not only to Ishumi District but also to Hanyu District and Nagara District to the north.<ref name="日">''Nihon Kodaishi Chimei Jiten'', p. 239.</ref>

Though this account in the ''Nihon Shoki'' cannot be taken at face value, the later ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'' entry for [[Jōgan (Japan)|Jōgan]] 9 ([[867]]), 4th month, 20th day mentions Kasugabe-no-Atai Kuronushime of Ishumi District, and so the establishment of a [[miyake]] is regarded as historical.<ref>''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 12: Chiba Prefecture'', p. 102.</ref><ref name="日"/> In such miyake establishment one can see the pattern of the [[Yamato Kingship]] driving wedges of directly administered territory into the lands of regional chieftains to extend its power.<ref>''Place Names of Chiba Prefecture'', p. 670.</ref> In the [[6th century]], the {{ill|Muzasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|武社国造|lt=Muzasa no Kuni no Miyatsuko}} — a branch of the [[Wani clan]], the [[maternal relatives]] of Kasuga-no-Yamada Empress — is also said to have advanced into the central part of the [[Kujūkuri Beach|Kujūkuri coast]] to the northeast,<ref>''Shoku Nihongi 4'', supplementary note 29-60.</ref> and the ''{{ill|Shoku Nihon Kōki|ja|続日本後紀}}'' entry for [[Jōwa (Japan)|Jōwa]] 2 ([[835]]), 3rd month, 16th day records the founding of {{ill|Sōsa District|ja|匝瑳郡|lt=Sōsa District}} in recognition of the achievements of {{ill|Mononobe-no-Ko-Koto|ja|物部小事|lt=Mononobe-no-Ko-Koto}}, the younger brother of {{ill|Mononobe-no-Ikoji|ja|物部木蓮子}} (and thus the uncle of [[Ankan]]'s other consort {{ill|Yakahime|ja|宅媛|lt=Yakahime}}).<ref>''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 12: Chiba Prefecture'', p. 495.</ref> This may reflect the decline of the local chieftain power in this region in the middle and late Kofun period.<ref name="千"/>

== Notes ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== Footnotes ===
{{Reflist|group=注}}
=== Citations ===
{{Reflist|2}}

== References ==
* Ogasawara Nagakazu (supervisor), ''Place Names of Chiba Prefecture (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 12)'', Heibonsha, 1996, ISBN 4-582-49012-3, pp. 670, 840.
* Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten Hensan Iinkai (ed.), ''Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 12: Chiba Prefecture'', Kadokawa Shoten, 1984, ISBN 4-04-001120-1, pp. 102, 495.
* Tenri University Press Editorial Committee, ''Sendai Kuji Hongi'' (Tenri Library Zenpon Sōsho, Washo-no-bu, Vol. 41), Tenri University Press, 1978, p. 632.
* Ogihara Asao (trans.), ''Nihon no Koten Kanyaku (1): Kojiki'', Shōgakukan, 1983, ISBN 4-09-556001-0, p. 37.
* Sakamoto Tarō et al., ''Nihon Shoki (3)'' (Iwanami Bunko), Iwanami Shoten, 1994, ISBN 4-00-300043-9, p. 214.
* Ujitani Tsutomu (trans.), ''Nihon Shoki (Jō): Zen Gendaigoyaku'' (Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko), Kōdansha, 1988, ISBN 4-06-158833-8, p. 370.
* Katō Kenkichi et al., ''Nihon Kodaishi Chimei Jiten'', [[Yūzankaku]], 2007, ISBN 978-4-639-01995-4, p. 239.
* Aoki Kazuo et al., ''Shoku Nihongi 4'', Iwanami Shoten, 1995, ISBN 4-00-240015-8, p. 529.

== See also ==
* {{ill|Nashiro|ja|名代}}

== External links ==
* [http://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/izi.html Walking the Ancient Tombs of Bōsō: The Tombs of Ijimi Province]

{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{デフォルトソート:いしみのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:上総国]]
[[Category:千葉県の歴史]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:伊甚国造]]
